Related Expert Advice articles

How to Choose a Backpacking Stove

Decide on the stove type you want

Backpacking stoves are categorized by the type of fuel they use and how the fuel is stored.
- Canister stoves use canisters of isobutane or propane. They’re easy to use and low maintenance.

use canisters of isobutane or propane. They’re easy to use and low maintenance.
- Integrated canister systems pair a canister with a burner and a built-in pot.

Integrated canister systems

pair a canister with a burner and a built-in pot.
- Liquid fuel stoves connect to refillable fuel bottles. They’re versatile because they burn many types of fuels like white gas, kerosene and unleaded auto fuel.

connect to refillable fuel bottles. They’re versatile because they burn many types of fuels like white gas, kerosene and unleaded auto fuel.
- Alternative-fuel stoves run on fuel pellets, wood or other fuel.

Alternative-fuel stoves

run on fuel pellets, wood or other fuel.

The pros and cons of each type
- Canister stoves. Pros: easy to use. Low maintenance. Boils quickly. Cons: flame can weaken in the cold.

Pros: easy to use. Low maintenance. Boils quickly. Cons: flame can weaken in the cold.
- Integrated canister systems. Pros: boil water extremely quickly. Cons: prone to tipping.

Integrated canister systems.

Pros: boil water extremely quickly. Cons: prone to tipping.
- Liquid fuel stoves. Pros: work well in cold weather. Boil water quickly, even in cold weather. Cons: require priming and occasional maintenance.

Pros: work well in cold weather. Boil water quickly, even in cold weather. Cons: require priming and occasional maintenance.
- Wood-Burning Stoves. Pros: often simple, lightweight. Cons: require finding dry wood. Fire bans may limit use.

Wood-Burning Stoves.

Pros: often simple, lightweight. Cons: require finding dry wood. Fire bans may limit use.
- Denatured Alcohol Stoves. Pros: simple, quiet, use cheap alcohol. Cons: slow to boil.

Denatured Alcohol Stoves.

Pros: simple, quiet, use cheap alcohol. Cons: slow to boil.

Select the right specs and features

Consider a stove’s weight, average time to boil one liter of water and whether it features a push-button spark igniter (found on some canister-fuel stoves).

Frequently asked questions about camp stoves

What are some popular brands for camp stoves at REI?

The top brands for camp stoves include:
- Coleman
- Zempire
- Primus
- GSI Outdoors
- Jetboil

Browse our selection of top outdoor gear and apparel brands.

How can I earn a Co-op Member Reward on my camp stoves purchase?

REI Co-op Members get 10% back annually on every eligible purchase, including most full-priced camp stoves. (10% is typical but not guaranteed.)

To earn or redeem rewards, buy a one-time $30 lifetime membership, or sign in as a member. See details about the Co-op Member Reward .

How much do camp stoves cost?

At REI Co-op, we carry a wide range of camp stoves that can start under $50 with some higher-end brands and models costing over $200.
